title: Welcome to Ulum al-Hadith
summary: Ulum al-Hadith is the field that teaches how hadith reports are studied carefully.
points: This path introduces simple terms step by step.
You will learn how hadith reports are described, transmitted, and checked.
Use this as a beginner study aid, not a replacement for qualified teachers.

title: What Is a Hadith?
summary: A hadith is a report about the Prophet Muhammad, peace be upon him.
points: It may mention his words, actions, approvals, or personal qualities.
Hadith helps Muslims learn the Sunnah.
Beginners should learn the terms before making conclusions.

title: Sunnah, Khabar, and Athar
summary: Sunnah refers to the Prophet's way. Khabar means report. Athar often refers to earlier Muslims.
points: Some scholars use Sunnah and Hadith closely together.
Khabar can be a broad word for transmitted reports.
Athar is commonly used for reports from Companions or Successors.

title: Qur'an, Qudsi, Matn, and Sanad
summary: The Qur'an is Allah's revealed Book. Hadith Qudsi is a sacred report narrated from Allah.
points: The matn is the wording or message of the hadith.
The sanad is the chain of narrators.
A rawi is a narrator found in that chain.

title: How Hadith Was Received
summary: Classical students received hadith through careful learning methods from teachers.
points: Al-sama means hearing directly from a teacher.
Al-qira'ah means reading to a teacher for correction.
Other methods include ijazah, munawalah, kitabah, i'lam, wijadah, and wasiyyah.

title: How Hadith Are Classified
summary: Hadith can be grouped by narrator type, number of narrators, and authenticity terms.
points: Narrator type includes qudsi, marfu, mawquf, and maqtu.
Number of narrators includes mutawatir and ahad.
Authenticity terms include sahih, hasan, and da'if.

title: Ahad: Mashhur, Aziz, Gharib
summary: Ahad reports are reports that do not reach the broad transmission level of mutawatir.
points: Mashhur has three or more narrators at a level, without reaching mutawatir.
Aziz has at least two narrators at a key level.
Gharib is reported through one narrator at a key level.

title: Mutawatir Reports
summary: Mutawatir refers to a report transmitted by many narrators across the generations.
points: The number is so large that they could not normally have agreed to invent it.
Mutawatir lafzi keeps the same wording.
Mutawatir ma'nawi shares the same meaning with different wording.

title: Sahih and Hasan
summary: Sahih and hasan are terms scholars use for strong or acceptable hadith reports.
points: Sahih includes a connected chain, upright and precise narrators, and no hidden defect.
Hasan is similar, but narrator precision may be slightly lower.
Beginners should not grade hadith independently.

title: Da'if Reports
summary: Da'if means a report is weak because it lacks one or more required conditions.
points: Weakness may relate to the chain, narrator information, or other hadith conditions.
Scholars discuss weak reports with care and conditions.
If a report is weak, that should be made clear to readers.

title: Mu'allaq Reports
summary: A mu'allaq report has narrator omission from the beginning of the chain.
points: One narrator, several narrators, or the whole beginning of the chain may be omitted.
This often appears in shortened references or chapter headings.
Students should notice when a chain is not fully shown.

title: Mursal Reports
summary: A mursal report is narrated by a Tabi'i directly from the Prophet without naming the Companion.
points: The Companion link is not mentioned in the chain.
Scholars discuss mursal reports carefully and with conditions.
This term teaches students to ask who is missing from the chain.

title: Mu'dal and Munqati'
summary: Mu'dal and munqati' are types of reports with disconnected chains.
points: Mu'dal usually means two or more narrators are missing in sequence.
Munqati' means the chain is broken or not continuous.
These categories require careful scholarly checking.

title: Mudallas Reports
summary: Tadlis means concealing something in the chain in a way that can make it look stronger.
points: A narrator may use unclear wording or an unusual name for a teacher.
Scholars study wording such as 'from' and how the narrator received the report.
This topic shows why exact transmission details matter.

title: Maudhu': Fabricated Reports
summary: Maudhu' means a report falsely attributed to the Prophet, peace be upon him.
points: Fabricated reports may appear because of politics, stories, or careless preaching.
Scholars warned strongly against attributing false words to the Prophet.
Specific religious claims should be checked before being shared.

title: Recognising Fabrication
summary: Scholars look at both chain and text when warning about fabricated reports.
points: Chain signs may include broken chains or known fabricators.
Text signs may include extreme rewards or meanings that clash with established Islam.
Beginners should not judge alone, but should learn why checking matters.

title: Shadh Reports
summary: A shadh report involves isolation or contradiction in a way scholars need to examine.
points: One common discussion is a narration conflicting with a stronger narration.
Scholars compare chains, narrators, and wording.
This topic shows that strong-looking reports still need careful comparison.

title: Mudraj Reports
summary: Mudraj means extra words were inserted and mistaken as part of the hadith.
points: The addition may appear in the text or chain.
It may come from a narrator's explanation or misunderstanding.
Scholars separate the Prophet's wording from added wording.

title: Maqlub Reports
summary: Maqlub involves reversal or switching in a chain or text.
points: A narrator's name may be switched or placed in the wrong order.
A word in the text may be reversed or changed by mistake.
This topic shows why exact names and wording matter.

title: Musalsal Reports
summary: Musalsal reports share a repeated pattern in transmission.
points: The shared pattern may be wording, action, time, place, or narrator state.
Hadith al-Rahmah is often used as an example of musalsal transmission.
This reminds students that hadith study also records how knowledge was passed on.
